json_data: An operating system (OS) is the executive manager of a computing system that manages all hardware and software.
There are 6 main managers of an OS: Memory manager, Processor manager, Job scheduler, Process scheduler, Device manager, and File manager.
The Memory Manager is in charge of main memory (RAM) and handles allocation, reallocation, and deallocation of memory space.
The Processor Manager decides how to allocate the CPU and monitors each process's transition.
Device Management is responsible for connecting with every device on the system and allocates devices based on scheduling policies.
File Management keeps track of every file in the system and enforces restrictions on file access control.
Network Management provides convenient way for authorized users to share resources and takes responsibility for network connectivity.
User Interface includes Graphical User Interface (GUI) and Command line interface.
First-generation computers (1940-1955) used vacuum tubes which failed frequently.
Second-generation computers (1955-1965) used transistors and introduced high-level programming languages like COBOL and FORTRAN.
Third-generation computers (mid 1960s to mid 1970s) used integrated circuits and timesharing.
Fourth-generation computers (1975-1991) employed very-large-scale integration (VLSI) and developed the first microprocessor (Intel 4004).
The first IBM PC was released in 1981 with Intel microprocessor and Microsoft operating system.
The first GUI was developed at Xerox Corporation's Palo Alto Research Center (PARC).
There are 4 categories of Operating Systems based on response time: Batch systems, Interactive systems, Real-time systems, and Hybrid systems.
UNIVAC was delivered to the U.S. Census Bureau in 1951 and predicted Eisenhower as the winner of the 1952 U.S. presidential election.
ENIAC was created by Dr. John Mauchly and J. Presper Eckert for use in the war.
The Stored Program Concept stores the computer program as well as data in the computer's memory.
A thread is a unit smaller than a process that can be scheduled and executed.
Multiprocessing is where two or more CPUs share the same main memory and I/O devices.
Symmetric multiprocessing allows several CPUs to process multiple jobs at the same time independently.
In asymmetric multiprocessing, some OS functions are assigned to subordinate processors which take instructions from the main CPU.
Cloud Computing allows OS to accommodate remote access to system resources and provides increased security for transactions.
